meta.title: Zambia CDC 2024 Chemistry Syllabus — Structured RAG Dataset
meta.source: Ministry of Education, Curriculum Development Centre, Lusaka, 2024
meta.purpose: Structured curriculum data for AI-assisted quiz, lesson plan, and assessment generation. Each record maps to one embeddable chunk aligned to official CDC competences.

specificCompetences: Demonstrate understanding of chemistry
learningActivities: Describing chemistry as a science that deals with the study of matter and its properties under different conditions
Investigating the main branches of chemistry such as inorganic chemistry, physical chemistry, organic chemistry, analytical chemistry and biochemistry
curriculum.expectedStandard: Understanding of chemistry and its branches demonstrated correctly

specificCompetences: Relate the importance of chemistry to everyday life
learningActivities: Exploring the importance of chemistry in everyday life
Researching on the role of chemistry in everyday life: personal care products, medicine
Evaluating the impact of chemical reactions and products on the environment and health
Designing solutions to real-world problems using chemistry
curriculum.expectedStandard: Importance of chemistry to everyday life related correctly

curriculum.subtopic: Safety and Waste Management in the Laboratory
specificCompetences: Apply laboratory safety
Manage waste in the laboratory
learningActivities: Applying safety practices in the laboratory such as precautions and first aid steps
Classifying laboratory waste: chemicals, glassware, paper
Managing laboratory waste handling and disposal
curriculum.expectedStandard: Laboratory safety applied correctly; waste managed appropriately

specificCompetences: Use apparatus and equipment in chemistry
Measure quantities precisely and accurately
learningActivities: Demonstrating the use of various laboratory apparatus and equipment for chemistry-related activities
Demonstrating accuracy and precision in measuring quantities such as time, temperature, mass/weight and volume
curriculum.expectedStandard: Laboratory apparatus used correctly; quantities measured with precision and accuracy

curriculum.subtopic: States and Basic Units of Matter
specificCompetences: Demonstrate understanding of states and basic units of matter
learningActivities: Investigating the states of matter: solids, liquids, gases, plasma
Exploring the basic units of matter: atoms, molecules, ions
curriculum.expectedStandard: States and basic units of matter demonstrated correctly

specificCompetences: Demonstrate understanding of the kinetic theory of matter
learningActivities: Exploring the principles of the kinetic theory of matter in terms of temperature and kinetic energy, motion and arrangement of particles, collisions, diffusion
Demonstrating the evidence of kinetic theory such as diffusion and Brownian motion
curriculum.expectedStandard: Kinetic theory of matter demonstrated correctly

curriculum.subtopic: Heating and Cooling Curves of Matter
specificCompetences: Construct the heating and cooling curves of matter
learningActivities: Constructing the heating and cooling curves
Interpreting heating and cooling curves
curriculum.expectedStandard: Heating and cooling curves constructed and interpreted correctly

specificCompetences: Demonstrate understanding of elements in everyday life
learningActivities: Describing an element as a substance consisting of only one type of atom
Classifying substances as elements: metallic and non-metallic elements in everyday life
Exploring the diagrammatic representation of elements
curriculum.expectedStandard: Understanding of elements demonstrated correctly

specificCompetences: Demonstrate understanding of compounds
learningActivities: Describing a compound as a substance made up of two or more elements chemically combined
Exploring various compounds in everyday life
Exploring the diagrammatic representation of elements, compounds and mixtures
Analysing the characteristics of compounds
curriculum.expectedStandard: Understanding of compounds demonstrated correctly

specificCompetences: Demonstrate understanding of mixtures
learningActivities: Describing a mixture as a substance consisting of two or more substances physically combined
Exploring examples of mixtures in everyday life
Classifying mixtures as homogeneous and heterogeneous
Exploring the diagrammatic representation of mixtures
Comparing and contrasting compounds and mixtures
curriculum.expectedStandard: Understanding of mixtures demonstrated correctly

specificCompetences: Differentiate physical and chemical changes
learningActivities: Exploring with examples the physical and chemical changes
Comparing and contrasting physical and chemical changes
curriculum.expectedStandard: Physical and chemical changes differentiated correctly

specificCompetences: Determine the purity of a substance
learningActivities: Determining the purity of a substance: melting point, boiling point, density, refractive index, chromatography, effects of impurities
Exploring the importance of pure versus impure substances in foodstuffs, drinks, salt, on icy roads, inhaled substances
curriculum.expectedStandard: Purity of substance determined correctly

specificCompetences: Separate mixtures into their desired components
learningActivities: Separating mixtures into their components using appropriate techniques: filtration, evaporation, simple distillation, fractional distillation, crystallisation, chromatography, separating funnel, centrifugation, sublimation, decantation
Investigating the applications and effects of separating techniques on the environment
curriculum.expectedStandard: Mixtures separated correctly using appropriate techniques

specificCompetences: Demonstrate understanding of an atom and its structure
learningActivities: Exploring atomic models (Bohr, Dalton model)
Describing an atom as the smallest particle of an element which takes part in a chemical reaction
Describing Bohr Model structure of an atom: nucleus at the centre surrounded by electrons
curriculum.expectedStandard: Understanding of an atom and its structure demonstrated correctly

specificCompetences: Explore the properties of the sub-atomic particles
learningActivities: Identifying sub-atomic particles: protons, neutrons and electrons
Analysing the relative charges and appropriate relative masses of protons, neutrons and electrons (charges: +1, 0, -1; masses: 1, 1, 1/1840)
Justifying the neutrality of an atom
curriculum.expectedStandard: Properties of sub-atomic particles explored correctly

curriculum.subtopic: Atomic Number, Mass Number and Nucleon Number
specificCompetences: Manipulate the numbers of sub-atomic particles
learningActivities: Describing atomic and mass numbers: (a) atomic number as the number of protons in an atom; (b) mass number as the sum of protons and neutrons in an atom
Formulating expressions relating to numbers of sub-atomic particles: A = Z + N
Chemical symbols and nuclide notation: interpreting and constructing the nuclide notation of atoms where X is atomic symbol, A is mass number and Z is atomic number
curriculum.expectedStandard: Numbers of sub-atomic particles manipulated correctly

specificCompetences: Interpret chemical symbols and nuclide notation
learningActivities: Exploring the chemical symbols of elements from the English, Latin, Greek names
Interpreting and constructing the nuclide notation of atoms where X is symbol, A is mass number and Z is atomic number
curriculum.expectedStandard: Chemical symbols and nuclide notation interpreted correctly

specificCompetences: Apply electronic configuration of atoms
learningActivities: Describing the electronic configuration as the arrangement of electrons in the shells of atoms
Demonstrating the build-up of electrons in shells for the first 20 elements using the formula 2n² taking into consideration the duplet and octet rules by drawing structures of atoms
Relating the electronic configurations of the first 20 elements to their positions on the Periodic Table
Classifying elements as metals or non-metals using their valence electrons and valency
Applying atomic structure concept to position elements on the periodic table
curriculum.expectedStandard: Electronic configuration of atoms applied correctly

specificCompetences: Determine relative atomic mass of an element from relative isotopic masses
learningActivities: Describing isotopes as atoms of the same element having the same number of protons but different mass numbers (nucleon numbers)
Determining the relative atomic mass of an element given the percentage abundances of isotopes and from mass spectrum
Exploring the uses of radioactive isotopes: medical treatment of cancer, industrial uses, as tracers in archaeology, nuclear energy production
Exploring the hazardous nature of radioactive isotopes: health risks, contamination of the environment, accidents and mutagens
Exploring the safe storage and handling of radioisotopes
curriculum.expectedStandard: Relative atomic mass of an element from relative isotopic masses determined correctly

specificCompetences: Demonstrate understanding of chemical bonding
learningActivities: Describing chemical bonding as a process by which atoms combine chemically to form molecules or compounds
Analysing the types of bonding: ionic (electrovalent), covalent, metallic
curriculum.expectedStandard: Understanding of chemical bonding demonstrated correctly

specificCompetences: Demonstrate understanding of ion formation in bonding
learningActivities: Describing an ion as an atom or chemically bonded group of atoms that have gained an overall electrical charge
Describing the formation of monatomic ions as cations by electron loss and anions by electron gain
Relating the charges of ions to their valencies
Relating the charges of radicals to their valencies: carbonate CO3²⁻, sulphate SO4²⁻, ammonium NH4⁺, nitrate NO3⁻, phosphate PO4³⁻
curriculum.expectedStandard: Ion formation in bonding demonstrated correctly

specificCompetences: Deduce chemical formulae of compounds
learningActivities: Assigning valencies to elements and groups of atoms/radicals
Formulating chemical formulae of compounds using (a) valencies (b) charges
Naming of chemical compounds: (a) binary and (b) ternary compounds
curriculum.expectedStandard: Chemical formulae of compounds deduced correctly

specificCompetences: Demonstrate understanding of ionic bonding
learningActivities: Describing ionic bond as an electrostatic force of attraction between cations and anions
Describing the formation of ionic/electrovalent compounds
Explaining the properties of ionic compounds: high melting and boiling points, high density, conducts electricity in molten state or solution, made of ions, solid at rtp
Exploring the uses of ionic compounds in everyday life
curriculum.expectedStandard: Understanding of ionic bonding demonstrated correctly

specificCompetences: Demonstrate understanding of covalent bonding
learningActivities: Describing covalent bond as a shared pair of electrons between non-metal atoms
Justifying formation of single, double and triple covalent bonds between atoms: hydrogen H₂, oxygen O₂ and nitrogen N₂ molecules respectively
Examining the properties of covalent compounds: low melting and boiling points, low density, do not conduct electricity in solid or solution form, made of molecules
Describing a molecule as a group of covalently bonded atoms which can exist independently
Exploring the uses of covalent compounds
curriculum.expectedStandard: Understanding of covalent bonding demonstrated correctly

specificCompetences: Demonstrate understanding of metallic bonding
learningActivities: Describing metallic bonding as the electrostatic force of attraction between positively charged ions and the pool of delocalised electrons within a metallic lattice
Exploring the uses of metallic substances
Justifying the use of specific metallic substances for different applications in everyday life
curriculum.expectedStandard: Understanding of metallic bonding demonstrated correctly

specificCompetences: Classify compounds into simple and giant structures
learningActivities: Describing structures of simple and giant compounds
Illustrating structures of simple and giant compounds
Distinguishing different compounds using the structures: simple and giant structures, macromolecules and ionic lattices
curriculum.expectedStandard: Compounds classified into simple and giant structures correctly

specificCompetences: Demonstrate understanding of the principles of chemical reactions
learningActivities: Describing a chemical reaction as a process in which a substance undergoes change to produce a new substance
Investigating the types of chemical reactions: (a) Direct combination/Synthesis, (b) Decomposition, (c) Single displacement, (d) Double displacement, (e) Chain reactions
curriculum.expectedStandard: Understanding of chemical reactions demonstrated correctly

specificCompetences: Construct chemical equations
learningActivities: Describing a chemical equation
Describing the notation of a chemical equation as: Reactants → Products
Constructing word equations from descriptive chemical changes
Formulating and balancing chemical equations with state symbols correctly written in lower case in the same line as the species not as subscripts
Constructing net ionic equations for balanced chemical reactions
Investigating chemical reactions as endothermic or exothermic
curriculum.expectedStandard: Chemical equations constructed and balanced correctly

specificCompetences: Demonstrate understanding of rates of chemical reactions
Investigate factors that affect the rates of chemical reactions
learningActivities: Describing the rate of a chemical reaction
Experimenting on the rates of chemical reactions
Applying rates of chemical reactions to real life situations
Exploring the factors that affect the rate of a chemical reaction: temperature, concentration, surface area, pressure, catalyst, light
Collecting and interpreting data on rates of chemical reactions — graph representations
Exploring methods of controlling rates of chemical reactions by manipulating factors that affect the rates of chemical reactions
curriculum.expectedStandard: Rates of chemical reactions demonstrated and factors identified correctly

specificCompetences: Interpret redox reactions
learningActivities: Analysing oxidation and reduction in terms of: (a) oxygen/hydrogen exchange, (b) electron transfer, (c) oxidation state changes
Examining a redox reaction as a reaction involving both oxidation and reduction
Identifying the characteristics of oxidising and reducing agents
Oxidising agents identified using: potassium iodide solution as agent in the presence of starch, acidified potassium iodide paper
Reducing agents identified using: acidified potassium dichromate, potassium permanganate as agents and observing colour changes
Determining oxidation numbers of elements with variable oxidation states
Deducing a redox reaction using oxidation numbers
Describing a non-redox reaction as one in which there is neither oxidation nor reduction involved
curriculum.expectedStandard: Redox reactions interpreted correctly

curriculum.subtopic: Composition of Acids, Bases and Salts
specificCompetences: Analyse the composition of acids, bases and salts
learningActivities: Investigating acids, bases, salts: chemical compositions of acids, bases and salts
curriculum.expectedStandard: Composition of acids, bases and salts analysed correctly

specificCompetences: Demonstrate understanding of acids
Analyse the properties of acids
learningActivities: Classifying acids in terms of: (a) Nature — organic and inorganic, (b) Strength — strong and weak
Describing basicity of an acid as the number of ionisable hydrogens present in one molecule of an acid: monobasic, dibasic and tribasic

Demonstrating the physical and chemical properties of acids: (a) Physical properties — sour taste, pH less than 7, electrolytes; (b) Chemical properties — reactions with metals, bases, carbonates, bicarbonates and effects on indicators; does not react with highly reactive metals such as potassium, sodium, calcium
Exploring uses of acids and their environmental impact
curriculum.expectedStandard: Understanding of acids demonstrated correctly; properties analysed

specificCompetences: Demonstrate understanding of bases
Analyse the properties of bases
learningActivities: Classifying bases in terms of solubility, further classifying the soluble ones as alkalis as strong and weak
Describing acidity of a base as the number of hydroxides present in a molecule: monoacidic, diacidic and triacidic

Demonstrating the physical and chemical properties of bases: (a) Physical properties — taste, pH greater than 7, texture, electrolytes, alkalinity; (b) Chemical properties — reactions with acids and ammonium salts, effects on indicators
Exploring the uses of bases and their environmental impact
Classifying oxides: basic oxides, acidic oxides, amphoteric oxides, neutral oxides
Exploring the uses of oxides
curriculum.expectedStandard: Understanding of bases demonstrated correctly; properties analysed

specificCompetences: Apply acid-base indicators to determine the acidity, alkalinity and neutrality of a substance
learningActivities: Describing acid-base indicators
Investigating types of acid-base indicators
Determining the acidity, alkalinity or neutrality of a substance using pH scale, litmus paper/solution, methyl orange, phenolphthalein, universal indicator paper/solution
Making acid-base indicators from locally available resources
curriculum.expectedStandard: Acid-base indicators applied correctly to determine pH

specificCompetences: Prepare and obtain salts
learningActivities: Classifying salts: normal salts, acidic and basic salts, hydrated and anhydrous salts

Preparing soluble salts using appropriate methods: reaction of acid with alkali (titration), reaction of acid with insoluble base, reaction of acid with metal carbonate, reaction of acid with a reactive metal
Preparing insoluble salts by precipitation: silver chloride, lead(II)
Obtaining salts from solutions by evaporation and crystallisation
Describing water of crystallisation
Discussing the importance of water of crystallisation to hydrated salts
Determining the solubility of salts: soluble and insoluble salts
Investigating the behaviour of salts with reference to the atmosphere: hygroscopic, efflorescent, deliquescent
Analysing the use of salts in real world scenarios: food preservation, medicine, agriculture
Designing a solution using salt to real world problems such as preserving food, purifying water
curriculum.expectedStandard: Salts prepared and obtained correctly

specificCompetences: Investigate the chemical composition of salts in terms of cations and anions present
learningActivities: Analysing and identifying cations and anions in their aqueous solutions: cations being aluminium Al³⁺, ammonium NH₄⁺, calcium Ca²⁺, copper(II) Cu²⁺, iron(II) Fe²⁺, iron(III) Fe³⁺ and zinc Zn²⁺ using aqueous sodium hydroxide and aqueous ammonia; anions being carbonate CO₃²⁻, chloride Cl⁻, iodide I⁻, nitrate NO₃⁻, sulphate SO₄²⁻
Carrying out chemical tests to identify gases: ammonia NH₃, carbon dioxide CO₂, chlorine Cl₂, hydrogen H₂, oxygen O₂, sulphur dioxide SO₂, water vapour H₂O
Exploring the use of analysis of salts in creation of economical products
Designing a new product that uses a new type of food preservative as fertiliser
curriculum.expectedStandard: Chemical composition of salts in terms of cations and anions identified correctly

curriculum.subtopic: Relative Formula Mass and Relative Molecular Mass
specificCompetences: Determine the relative formula mass and relative molecular mass
learningActivities: Describing the relative atomic mass
Calculating the relative atomic mass of isotopes
Describing the relative molecular mass
Determining the relative formula mass and relative molecular mass
curriculum.expectedStandard: Relative formula mass and relative molecular mass determined correctly

specificCompetences: Apply the concept of mass composition and percentage composition
learningActivities: Determining the mass composition and percentage composition of elements in a compound
curriculum.expectedStandard: Mass composition and percentage composition applied correctly

specificCompetences: Determine the number of moles of a given substance
learningActivities: Describing the mole as the amount of substance that contains 6.0 × 10²³ (Avogadro's constant) particles (atoms, ions, molecules, electrons, protons, neutrons)

Determining the number of moles using: (a) Number of particles in relation to Avogadro's constant, (b) Physical masses (m) and molar mass (MM), (c) Physical volume (V) and molar volume (Vm) of any gas at r.t.p. and s.t.p. where Vm = 24 dm³ at r.t.p. and 22.4 dm³ at s.t.p.
curriculum.expectedStandard: Number of moles determined correctly

specificCompetences: Determine empirical and molecular formula
learningActivities: Describing empirical and molecular formula
Determining the empirical formula of a compound given: (a) the molecular formula using ratios, (b) percentage composition by mass, (c) masses
Determining the molecular formula from empirical formula using MF = (EF)n
curriculum.expectedStandard: Empirical and molecular formula determined correctly

specificCompetences: Demonstrate understanding of concentration of solutions
learningActivities: Describing the concentration of a solution in terms of: (a) mass concentration as the mass of a solute per unit volume of a solution (g/dm³), (b) mole concentration (molarity, M) as the number of moles per unit volume of solute per unit volume of solution (mol/dm³)
Applying the dilution law (M₁V₁ = M₂V₂)
Determining concentration given the amount of solute and volume of a solution and vice versa: M = n/v, mass concentration = mass/volume
curriculum.expectedStandard: Understanding of concentration of solutions demonstrated correctly

specificCompetences: Prepare standard solutions
learningActivities: Describing a standard solution
Preparing standard solutions starting with soluble solids and concentrated solutions
Calculating specific gravity (density) and % purity
curriculum.expectedStandard: Standard solutions prepared suitably and correctly

specificCompetences: Carry out calculations involving stoichiometric reacting quantities
Determine the number of moles in hydrates
Determine percentage yield
Determine percentage purity
learningActivities: Describing reacting quantities
Carrying out calculations involving stoichiometric reacting quantities (moles, masses, volumes of gases and volumetric analysis/titration)
Determining the number of moles n in hydrates (X.nH₂O) by: (a) gravimetric analysis (solids), (b) volumetric analysis (liquids)
Determining percentage yield: % yield = (actual yield/theoretical yield) × 100
Determining percentage yield and purity: % Purity = (amount of component/amount of mixture) × 100
curriculum.expectedStandard: Stoichiometric reacting quantities, percentage yield and purity calculated correctly

specificCompetences: Solve environmental problems using stoichiometric principles
Explore the benefits of atom economy
learningActivities: Exploring the environmental stoichiometry: study of the balance of chemical elements and compounds in the environment in relation to the composition of clean air (nitrogen 78%, oxygen 20%, carbon dioxide 0.03%, rare gases 1%, water with variable percentage)
Exploring the environmental impact of chemical reactions
Solving environmental problems using stoichiometric principles
Describing atom economy as the measure of efficiency of chemical reactions or processes: Atom economy = (molecular mass of desired products/sum of molecular masses of all reactants) × 100
Exploring the benefits of atom economy: (a) reduced waste and bi-product, (b) minimised environmental impact, (c) lower resource consumption
curriculum.expectedStandard: Stoichiometric principles used to solve environmental problems; atom economy explored

specificCompetences: Demonstrate understanding of thermal reactions
Determine the enthalpy change of endothermic and exothermic reactions
learningActivities: Describing thermochemistry as the study of heat energy changes associated with chemical reactions and physical changes

Investigating characteristics of endothermic and exothermic reactions: enthalpy changes, energy profile diagrams (catalysed and uncatalysed), determining enthalpy changes using bond energies, temperature changes and natural processes such as respiration and photosynthesis
Determining enthalpy change related to endothermic and exothermic reactions in relation to bond breaking and bond formation using bond energies and temperature changes
curriculum.expectedStandard: Understanding of thermal reactions demonstrated; enthalpy change determined correctly

specificCompetences: Justify the need to harness alternative sources of energy
learningActivities: Exploring the advantages and disadvantages of sources of energy in terms of safety, cost, renewable/non-renewable, clean and unclean
Exploring the effects of using energy on the environment: air pollution, greenhouse effect, global warming
curriculum.expectedStandard: Need to harness alternative sources of energy justified

specificCompetences: Demonstrate understanding of nuclear energy
learningActivities: Discussing nuclear energy as energy released from the nucleus of a radioactive atom through fission and fusion
Analysing case studies of nuclear power plants including their design, operation and safety features
Evaluating the advantages and disadvantages of nuclear energy
Designing solutions to challenges related to nuclear energy such as safety concerns or waste disposal
Analysing the uses of nuclear energy: medical, industry, electricity generation
curriculum.expectedStandard: Understanding of nuclear energy demonstrated

specificCompetences: Demonstrate understanding of chemical equilibrium
learningActivities: Describing chemical equilibrium in a reversible chemical reaction where the rate of the forward reaction is equal to the rate of backward reaction, resulting in constant concentrations of the products and reactants over time
curriculum.expectedStandard: Understanding of chemical equilibrium demonstrated correctly

specificCompetences: Demonstrate reversibility of chemical reactions
learningActivities: Demonstrating the reversibility of chemical reactions that proceed both forward and backwards
Exploring reversibility of chemical reactions
Demonstrating thermal dissociation of ammonium salts
curriculum.expectedStandard: Reversibility of chemical reactions demonstrated correctly

specificCompetences: Analyse the characteristics of a chemical reaction in a dynamic equilibrium
Evaluate the factors that affect position of dynamic equilibrium
learningActivities: Describing a dynamic equilibrium
Exploring the characteristics of chemical reaction in dynamic equilibrium: (a) rates of forward and backward reactions are equal; (b) the amounts of reactants and products are constant
Investigating the factors that affect the position of equilibrium: temperature, concentration, pressure — applying Le Chatelier's principle; refer to Haber process
curriculum.expectedStandard: Dynamic equilibrium characteristics analysed; factors affecting equilibrium evaluated

specificCompetences: Demonstrate understanding of electrochemistry
learningActivities: Describing electrochemistry as the relationship between electricity and chemical reactions
curriculum.expectedStandard: Understanding of electrochemistry demonstrated correctly

specificCompetences: Evaluate the properties and applications of conductors and non-conductors
learningActivities: Investigating electrical conductivity of various materials: metals and non-metals
Evaluating the properties and applications of conductors and non-conductors
Classifying electrical conductivity of various materials: metals and non-metals
Researching on real world applications of conductors and non-conductors: electronics, transportation, medicine
curriculum.expectedStandard: Properties and applications of conductors and non-conductors evaluated correctly

specificCompetences: Demonstrate understanding of electrolytic processes
learningActivities: Describing electrolysis as the process that uses electric current to drive a chemical reaction at electrodes

Analysing solutions of compounds as electrolytes and non-electrolytes: solutions of strong acids, alkalis and salts; weak electrolytes — solutions of weak acids and bases; non-electrolytes — ionic compounds in solution, molten and covalent compounds

Investigating the electrolytic decomposition of molten binary ionic compounds: lead(II) bromide, aluminium oxide — referring to the half electrode reactions; metals to be produced at cathode by reduction and non-metals to be produced at anode by oxidation
Deducing the electrode products given a suitable electrolyte and electrodes
Discussing the electrochemical series as the arrangement of ions in order of their electrode potential

Applying the electrochemical series in the preferential discharge of ions in electrolysis: (a) dilute sulphuric acid H₂SO₄, (b) concentrated hydrochloric acid HCl, (c) copper(II) sulphate CuSO₄, (d) concentrated sodium chloride brine NaCl
curriculum.expectedStandard: Electrolytic processes demonstrated correctly

specificCompetences: Determine quantities of electrolytic products
learningActivities: Applying Faraday's Laws to determine the quantities of electrolytic products at the electrodes and the ionic charge
curriculum.expectedStandard: Quantities of electrolytic products determined correctly

specificCompetences: Analyse the uses of electrolytic processes in industries
learningActivities: Exploring the applications of electrolysis: extraction of metals (aluminium), electroplating, purification of metals (copper), anodising, chemical manufacture (H₂SO₄, NaOH, Cl₂, H₂)
curriculum.expectedStandard: Uses of electrolytic processes in industries analysed correctly

specificCompetences: Construct electrolytic cells
learningActivities: Constructing an electric cell by connecting two different electrodes dipped in suitable electrolyte to produce electricity
Relating reactivity series of metals to the amount of electricity produced by a simple cell

Comparing and contrasting simple cells and electrolytic cells in terms of similarities such as oxidation at the anode and reduction at cathode and differences such as polarities, charges and nature of electrodes
curriculum.expectedStandard: Electrolytic cells constructed correctly; compared with simple cells

specificCompetences: Demonstrate understanding of Groups and Periods of the Periodic Table
Apply periodic trends to determine metallic and non-metallic nature of elements
Apply periodic trends to predict the properties of newly discovered elements
learningActivities: Analysing trends in Periods 2 and 3 and various Groups I and VII in terms of physical and chemical properties: (a) Physical properties — solubility, melting and boiling points, density, colour, state of matter, atomic size; (b) Chemical properties — reactivity, electropositive, electronegative
Determining metallic and non-metallic nature using periodic trends
Researching on trends of elements in the same group/period as the new element
Predicting the properties of an element with a specific atomic number using the periodic trends
Conducting collaborative research project to investigate the discovery of new elements and their periodic trends
Exploring the importance of halogens: fluorine in toothpaste, chlorine in water treatment and antiseptics
Exploring the uses of the noble gases in providing an inert atmosphere: argon/neon/krypton in lighting, argon and krypton in insulation, xenon in medical anaesthesia, argon and helium in welding
Examining harmful effects of halogens and their compounds on the environment: CFCs and ozone layer depletion
Designing sustainable solutions to reduce the environmental impact of halogen compounds
curriculum.expectedStandard: Periodic trends understood; properties predicted correctly

specificCompetences: Investigate properties and uses of transition elements
learningActivities: Exploring the typical properties of transition metals: high density, high melting and boiling points, coloured compounds (iron(II) green, iron(III) brown, copper blue), variable valencies and forming complex ions
Conducting experiments to investigate the properties of transition elements: reactivity, catalytic activity
Exploring the uses of transition metals: catalysts, alloys, construction
curriculum.expectedStandard: Properties and uses of transition elements investigated correctly

curriculum.subtopic: Properties of Metals and Reactivity Series
specificCompetences: Investigate physical and chemical properties of metals
Determine the reactivity series of metals
learningActivities: Describing a metal as a pure substance that ionises by losing electrons
Investigating physical properties of metals: thermal and electrical conductivity, sonority, malleability, ductility, lustre, density, melting and boiling points, tensile strength
Investigating chemical properties of metals: reactivity with dilute acid, water, steam, oxygen, displacement reactions with other metals
Determining the reactivity series of metals using dilute acids, water, steam, oxygen/air, displacement reactions and simple cells
Analysing data from experiments involving the reactivity series of metals including identifying patterns and trends
curriculum.expectedStandard: Physical and chemical properties of metals investigated; reactivity series determined correctly

specificCompetences: Demonstrate understanding of metallurgy
Identify different mineral ores in the local environment
learningActivities: Discussing metallurgy as the science and technology of metals focusing on the occurrence, properties, extraction, processing and their uses
Exploring occurrence of ores in the local environment
Identifying different mineral ores using their physical and chemical properties
Classifying different mineral ores using their physical and chemical properties
Researching on the sources of metals: copper, gold, emeralds, nickel, lithium, cobalt, iron, aluminium, manganese
curriculum.expectedStandard: Metallurgy understood; mineral ores identified and classified correctly

specificCompetences: Demonstrate understanding of metal extraction
learningActivities: Discussing the sustainable ways of extracting metals: copper, iron, zinc by chemical reduction and aluminium by electrolytic reduction
Illustrating metal extraction process for a specific ore including the steps involved and equipment needed
curriculum.expectedStandard: Understanding of metal extraction demonstrated correctly

specificCompetences: Analyse the uses of metals in everyday life
learningActivities: Analysing the various uses of metals in everyday life: construction and building materials (steel, aluminium), transportation (airplanes, bicycles), electronics and technology (copper wiring, aluminium casings), packaging and consumer goods (aluminium cans, steel containers)
Developing sustainable use practices in a specific context including strategies for reducing waste and promoting recovering/reusing/recycling
Investigating the harmful effects of metals: lead poisoning (brain damage, infertility), sodium (raising high blood pressure, Alzheimer's), mercury (brain and kidney damage)
curriculum.expectedStandard: Uses of metals in everyday life analysed correctly

specificCompetences: Design an alloy for a specific application
learningActivities: Describing alloys as mixtures of two or more metals or non-metals: brass, bronze, steel
Drawing the structural representation of alloys with different nuclei positive in a sea of delocalised electrons
Explaining the advantages of using alloys over pure metals: conductivity, strength, weight, hardness, resistance to corrosion
Relating the uses of alloys to their properties: cutlery, packaging, aircraft parts
Designing an alloy for a specific application referring to composition, processing and properties
curriculum.expectedStandard: Alloy designed for specific application correctly

specificCompetences: Prevent corrosion of metals
learningActivities: Describing corrosion as the chemical wearing of metal by oxidation using atmospheric oxygen in the presence of moisture
Demonstrating corrosion — rusting of iron
Relating corrosion to the reactivity of metals
Applying different methods of preventing corrosion: sacrificial protection, painting, greasing/oiling, alloying and galvanising
curriculum.expectedStandard: Corrosion of metals prevented correctly

specificCompetences: Investigate the physical and chemical properties of non-metals
learningActivities: Describing non-metals as substances that ionise by gaining electrons (except hydrogen)
Investigating the physical properties of non-metals: density, melting and boiling points, thermal and electrical conductivity, physical state at rtp
Investigating the chemical properties of non-metals
Demonstrating use of physical and chemical properties of non-metals to solve problems, identify unknown substances or predict chemical reactions
curriculum.expectedStandard: Physical and chemical properties of non-metals investigated correctly

specificCompetences: Prepare hydrogen gas
Investigate the industrial manufacture of hydrogen
learningActivities: Preparing, collecting and testing hydrogen gas in laboratory by action of moderately reactive metals with water, steam and acids; collect by upward delivery; test — put lighted splint with a 'pop' sound
Demonstrating the physical and chemical properties of hydrogen gas (CWS L): colourless, without smell/odour, lighter than air, solubility
Exploring the industrial manufacture of hydrogen: cracking, electrolysis of water/brine, and from natural gas
Exploring the uses of hydrogen: reducing agent, fuel for rockets, manufacture of ammonia and margarine, welding
curriculum.expectedStandard: Hydrogen gas prepared, collected and tested correctly; industrial manufacture investigated

specificCompetences: Prepare oxygen gas
Investigate the industrial manufacture of oxygen
Investigate the properties and uses of oxygen gas
learningActivities: Preparing, collecting and testing oxygen gas in laboratory: catalytic decomposition of hydrogen peroxide, and thermal/catalytic decomposition of potassium chlorate; collect above water; test — relight a glowing splint
Exploring the industrial manufacture of oxygen: fractional distillation of air
Analysing the physical and chemical properties of oxygen gas: colour, odour, solubility, combustion
Exploring the uses of oxygen in industrial and natural processes: combustion, welding, in blast furnace, respiration
Practising sustainable ways of protecting the ozone layer
Demonstrating environmental awareness and concern for impact of human activities on the ozone layer
curriculum.expectedStandard: Oxygen gas prepared, collected and tested correctly; properties and uses investigated

specificCompetences: Prepare ammonia gas in the laboratory
Investigate the industrial manufacture of nitrogen and its related products
learningActivities: Preparing, collecting and testing ammonia gas in laboratory by action of base on an ammonium salt; collected by upward delivery; test — turns red litmus paper blue
Investigating the physical and chemical properties of ammonia: colour, odour, density/weight, solubility, as reducing agent, as a base/alkali, complexing reagent
Exploring the industrial manufacture of nitrogen: fractional distillation of air
Exploring the industrial manufacture of ammonia: Haber process
Exploring the characteristics of nitrogen: non-reactive, insoluble gas
Analysing the uses of nitrogen: as refrigerant, in packaging, in manufacture of ammonia gas
curriculum.expectedStandard: Ammonia gas prepared, collected and tested correctly; industrial manufacture investigated

specificCompetences: Prepare chlorine gas and its associated products
learningActivities: Preparing, collecting and testing chlorine gas by reaction of hot concentrated hydrochloric acid on manganese(IV) oxide; collected by downward delivery; test — turns damp blue litmus paper red then bleaches it
Investigating the physical and chemical properties of chlorine: colour, odour, density, solubility, poisonous, reactions with iron, non-metals, H₂, SO₂, iron(II) salts, halides
Exploring the uses of chlorine: sterilising water, manufacture of PVC, HCl, bleaching agents
Exploring the industrial manufacture of chlorine: electrolysis of brine NaCl
Preparing, collecting and testing hydrogen chloride by action of concentrated sulphuric acid on solid metallic halides; collected by downward delivery; reacts with ammonia to produce white smoke
Investigating the physical and chemical properties of hydrogen chloride gas: colour, odour, density, solubility, poisonous, reaction with ammonia and water
Preparing hydrochloric acid by dissolving hydrogen chloride gas in water
Exploring the uses of hydrochloric acid: manufacture of fertilisers, metal pickling, water treatment, as typical household cleaning agent
curriculum.expectedStandard: Chlorine gas prepared, collected and tested correctly; properties and uses explored

specificCompetences: Prepare sulphur dioxide
learningActivities: Investigating the formation of sulphur dioxide: combustion of sulphur, fossil fuels

Preparing, collecting and testing sulphur dioxide in laboratory by action of dilute acids on sulphites; collected by downward delivery; test — acidified potassium dichromate(V) turns green, decolourises purple potassium manganate(VII)
Investigating the physical and chemical properties of sulphur dioxide: colour, odour, density, solubility, poisonous, reaction with water, action on indicators, as a reducing agent
Exploring the uses of sulphur dioxide: food preservation, bleaching wood pulp for paper, manufacture of sulphuric acid
Investigating the impact of sulphur dioxide on the environment: air pollution, acid rain, damage to infrastructure, climate change, respiratory effects on humans
Exploring sustainable ways of reducing emissions of sulphur dioxide
Exploring the industrial manufacture of sulphuric acid: Contact process
Exploring the environmental impact of Contact process: pollution, resource depletion, water pollution, greenhouse gas emissions
Exploring the uses of sulphuric acid: explosives, drying agent, making salts, fertilisers
curriculum.expectedStandard: Sulphur dioxide prepared, collected and tested correctly; industrial manufacture and environmental impact explored

specificCompetences: Explore the nature and uses of carbon and its products
learningActivities: Discussing allotropes of carbon: crystalline graphite, diamond, non-crystalline coke, coal, soot, charcoal
Exploring the physical properties of allotropes of carbon and their uses
Investigating the formation and properties of carbon monoxide by incomplete combustion of carbon/carbon compounds, reduction of carbon dioxide by carbon
Preparing, collecting and testing carbon dioxide by reacting dilute acids with carbonates or bicarbonates; collected by downward delivery; test — forms white precipitate with lime water
Exploring the physical and chemical properties of carbon dioxide: colour, odour, density, solubility, reaction with limewater, alkalis, water, and carbon
Exploring the uses of carbon dioxide: fire extinguisher, carbonated drinks, dry ice, baking, photosynthesis
Analysing uses of limestone/calcium carbonate: manufacture of lime, glass, iron, tiles
Exploring the manufacturing of lime from limestone by thermal dissociation of limestone
Exploring the uses of lime and slaked lime: neutralising acidic soil, lime as a drying agent for ammonia
curriculum.expectedStandard: Nature and uses of carbon and its products explored correctly

specificCompetences: Investigate the nature and uses of silicon and its products
learningActivities: Describing silicon as a metalloid
Exploring the uses of silicon in semiconductors, transistors, diodes and capacitors

Comparing and contrasting fire resistance of silicone plastics to carbon-based macromolecules: nature of combustion products — silicones produce silicon dioxide/sand (SiO₂) while organic-based macromolecules produce carbon dioxide (CO₂)
Exploring the uses of silicones: silicon chips, artificial hips, breasts, oils, waxes, plastics
Investigating silicones as macromolecules that exist as oils, waxes or plastics and their structures
Exploring forms of silicon dioxide: sand, silica, quartz and their uses: making glass, as fire extinguisher, iron extraction, construction
curriculum.expectedStandard: Nature and uses of silicon and its products investigated correctly

specificCompetences: Demonstrate the understanding of organic chemistry
learningActivities: Describing organic chemistry
Describing organic compounds
Exploring the characteristics of homologous series: general molecular formula, functional group, physical and chemical properties, nomenclature
curriculum.expectedStandard: Understanding of organic chemistry demonstrated correctly

specificCompetences: Evaluate the nature and uses of saturated and unsaturated hydrocarbons
learningActivities: Illustrating the structures of aliphatic alkanes up to ten carbon atoms
Naming the structures of aliphatic alkanes up to ten carbon atoms — IUPAC system, general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂
Demonstrating the structures of alkane isomers and their uses; branched and unbranched — butane and pentane — IUPAC nomenclature
Illustrating and naming the structures of aliphatic alkenes and alkynes up to ten carbon atoms — general formula CₙH₂ₙ and CₙH₂ₙ₋₂ — IUPAC system ending 'ene' and 'yne'
Demonstrating the structures of alkene and alkyne positional isomers and their names — branched and unbranched butene, butyne, pentene, pentyne — IUPAC system
Exploring the preparation of alkanes, alkenes and alkynes (refer to methane, ethane, ethyne)
Exploring the non-apparent reactivity of alkanes as compared to other organic compounds
Investigating physical and chemical properties of alkanes and alkenes
Testing for unsaturation: decolourises bromine water rapidly
Exploring fractional distillation of petroleum/crude oil and fractions and their uses
Evaluating the environmental impact of fractional distillation of crude oil: air pollution, water pollution, waste management, resource depletion
Exploring ways of sustainable production of crude oil
Investigating the environmental impact of combustion of hydrocarbons: air and water pollution, greenhouse gas emissions, climate change, acid rain impact
Exploring sustainable ways of addressing combustion of hydrocarbons: carbon capture
curriculum.expectedStandard: Nature and uses of saturated and unsaturated hydrocarbons evaluated correctly

specificCompetences: Demonstrate understanding of carboxylic acids
Explore the preparation of alcohols
learningActivities: Illustrating and naming structures of carboxylic acids up to five carbon atoms; general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₁COOH
Exploring the preparation of carboxylic acids: ethanoic acid by oxidation of ethanol, hydrolysis of esters

Investigating physical and chemical properties of carboxylic acids: (a) physical — density, solubility, state of matter, boiling and melting points; (b) chemical — reactions with carbonates, metals and alcohols
Exploring the uses of carboxylic acids: food flavouring, esterification, manufacturing, in drinks
Describing alcohols
Exploring different types of alcohols
Illustrating the structures of alcohols up to five carbon atoms
Preparing alcohols: ethanol by hydration of ethene, fermentation of sugar, hydrolysis of esters

Investigating physical and chemical properties of alcohols: (a) physical — density, solubility, state of matter, boiling and melting points; (b) chemical — combustion, esterification, dehydration and oxidation
Exploring the uses of alcohols: as a fuel, as solvents, disinfectants, beer and wine, drug manufacturing

specificCompetences: Demonstrate understanding of esters
Prepare esters (alkyl alkanoates) for different uses
learningActivities: Describing esters
Illustrating structures of esters up to five carbon atoms: ester link, general formula CₘH₂ₘ₊₁C(=O)-O-CₙH₂ₙ₊₁
Naming structures of esters up to five carbon atoms ending with '-oate'
Exploring the preparation of esters by esterification
Investigating physical and chemical properties of esters: (a) physical — density, solubility, state of matter, boiling and melting points; (b) chemical — combustion and hydrolysis
Exploring the uses of esters: perfumes, flavouring and preservation

specificCompetences: Demonstrate understanding of macromolecules
learningActivities: Describing macromolecules as giant molecules formed by combination of many small molecules (monomers)
Exploring macromolecules and their formations: synthetic macromolecules — polyethene, PVC, polypropene, polystyrene, PTFE (Teflon), nylon, terylene; natural macromolecules — fats, starch and proteins
Analysing the formation of macromolecules as addition polymers and condensation polymers
Investigating the nature of plastics as thermoplastics and thermosetting
Comparing and contrasting structures of macromolecules: nylon, proteins, terylene
Exploring the typical uses of plastics and synthetic fibres: carrier bags, buckets, pipes, clothing, tents, strings/ropes
Investigating the biodegradability of polymers and their effects on the environment
Investigating the hydrolysis of fats, proteins and starch
Demonstrating the formation of soaps (saponification) and glycerine by alkali and acid hydrolysis respectively

specificCompetences: Analyse the role of chemistry in sustaining the environment
learningActivities: Discussing environmental chemistry as the study of chemical processes in the environment and how human and biological activities (anthropological activities) affect them
Researching on the impact of human activities on the environment and the role of chemistry in addressing these issues
Analysing the role of chemistry in environmental sustainability

specificCompetences: Investigate pollution control and mitigation
learningActivities: Investigating types of pollution: air, water, land

specificCompetences: Investigate the role of chemistry in sustainable agriculture
learningActivities: Investigating the use of sustainable agricultural practices: green pesticides, integrated bio-waste management, crop rotation, intercropping techniques, enhancing agroforestry, liming soil
curriculum.expectedStandard: Role of chemistry in sustainable agriculture investigated correctly

specificCompetences: Demonstrate understanding of climate change
Investigate causes and effects of climate change
learningActivities: Describing climate change as long-term changes in temperatures and weather patterns; note climate effects are regional/specific around the world
Exploring the causes of climate change: deforestation, industrial processes, fossil fuels
Analysing the effects of climate change: depletion of ozone layer, global warming, floods, drought

specificCompetences: Demonstrate understanding of water management
learningActivities: Describing water management as the management of water resources under set standards, policies and regulations
